topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights hummed a low, irritating frequency against my eyelids. I tried to focus on the texture of the canvas in the corner, but the edges of my vision kept blurring, swimming like oil on water. A strange, heavy warmth settled in my limbs, pulling me down toward the velvet chaise lounge. Then I noticed it—my latest abstract piece, hanging prominently near the entrance. It was displayed, bathed in that harsh white illumination, and I hadn't even seen the notice. A slow, profound droop began in my shoulders. "Oh, bother," I mumbled, my voice thick and slow, already feeling the pull of unconsciousness. I managed to lift a hand, but the movement felt like wading through deep water.

[story 2]

The air in the studio felt thick, heavy with the scent of turpentine and old coffee. My head felt like a lead weight, pressing down against my temples. I was supposed to be reviewing the catalogue, but the words swam. When I finally turned toward the main hall, my gaze snagged on my work—a vibrant chaos of color—mounted on a pedestal. It was there. Uninvited. A strange, languid wave washed over me, making my limbs feel incredibly pliable, as if gravity had suddenly doubled its pull. I blinked several times, the effort feeling monumental, and a soft, involuntary sigh escaped my lips. I leaned against the doorframe, the wood cool against my cheek, letting my eyes drift closed.

[story 3]

Every sound in the room seemed muffled, distant, as if submerged underwater. My eyelids felt weighted with sand, making the act of opening them a deliberate, arduous struggle. I wandered toward the display area, drawn by an inexplicable inertia. There it was: my painting, set apart from the rest, under the scrutiny of strangers. A deep, pervasive weariness began to seep into my bones, a slow, inevitable surrender. I stopped just short of the velvet rope, my breath catching in my chest, a quiet acknowledgment of the exhaustion claiming me. "Well," I whispered, the sound barely audible, a gentle relinquishing of control.
